quality_event_type_master_table.escalation_triggers: If the failure could have allowed non-conforming product to be shipped to the customer.
quality_event_type_master_table.technology_specific_notes: Applies to all measurement devices: spectrophotometers, gloss meters, film thickness gauges, thermocouples, pressure gauges, etc.
quality_event_type_master_table.classification_model_mapping: Quality System Nonconformance -> Triggers product containment.

quality_event_type_master_table.definition: A formal decision to authorize the reprocessing of non-conforming material to make it conform to the original specification.
quality_event_type_master_table.typical_trigger: An NCR is created for a defect that is deemed correctable.
quality_event_type_master_table.typical_disposition_path: The part is sent to a dedicated rework area with specific, documented instructions.
quality_event_type_master_table.escalation_triggers: If rework rates exceed a defined threshold, triggering a formal investigation into the root cause of the initial defects.
quality_event_type_master_table.technology_specific_notes: Rework must follow a 'separate rework-specific process control sheet' and avoid issues like the 'wrinkle zone' in thermoset coatings.
quality_event_type_master_table.classification_model_mapping: This is a disposition step within the NCR workflow, not a classification itself.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: Is an abnormality (product, process, reading) observed?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Yes: Proceed to Gate 2. No: End process, continue production.
decision_gate_reference_table.who_decides: Operator, Inspector, or Automated AI Vision System.
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: Visual check against standard, gauge reading, process parameter display (e.g., PLC screen), or automated inspection report.
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: At the point of discovery; immediate.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: No significant variation. This is the universal first step for any potential issue.
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This is the entry point into the quality system. The goal is to flag any potential deviation from the norm for further review, not to make a final judgment.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: Is this a confirmed deviation from a known standard or specification?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Yes: It is a Nonconformance. Proceed to Gate 3. No/Unsure: Classify as 'Observation' or 'Quality Alert'. Document for awareness and trend monitoring, then end formal process.
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: Product specification, control plan limits, boundary/limit samples, visual aid documents.
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: Within minutes of initial discovery and escalation.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: Boundary samples are critical for subjective appearance defects in liquid and powder coating (e.g., orange peel, gloss). For e-coat, this may involve checking lab results against bath chemistry specs.
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This gate separates minor observations from formal nonconformances. A common mistake is to bypass this and either over-report minor issues or under-report significant ones. A 'Quality Alert' is an effective tool for handling the 'No/Unsure' path.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: Does the nonconformance affect a customer-designated Safety or Critical Characteristic (CC)?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Yes: Classify as 'Critical NCR'. Immediately escalate to Quality Manager, contain all suspect stock, and prepare for customer notification. No: Proceed to Gate 4.
decision_gate_reference_table.who_decides: Operator, Supervisor, or Quality Engineer (all personnel must be trained to identify CCs).
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: Part drawing with CC symbols (e.g., Ford's inverted delta), Control Plan identifying special characteristics.
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: Immediate, as part of the initial nonconformance assessment.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: For coatings, a CC might relate to a minimum film thickness required for corrosion protection or the absence of specific prohibited substances.
decision_gate_reference_table.standard_basis: Ford Q1 Requirements; IATF 16949 Clause 8.3.3.3 (Special characteristics).
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This is a critical, non-negotiable gate. Any nonconformance on a CC automatically triggers the highest level of internal response. Failure to do so is a major audit finding.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: Is the nonconformance related to the product itself, or is it a process parameter deviation with no known product impact yet?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Product: Classify as 'Product NCR'. Quarantine the physical product and proceed to Disposition (Gate 5). Process: Classify as 'Process NCR'. Correct the process parameter and initiate an investigation to determine if any product needs to be contained.
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: Physical defect on a part versus an out-of-spec reading on a process controller (e.g., oven temperature, bath pH).
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: At the point of formal NCR creation.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: For e-coat, a bath chemistry deviation (Process NCR) often requires automatically placing all product on hold since the last known good check, effectively treating it as a potential Product NCR.
decision_gate_reference_table.standard_basis: AIAG CQI-12 (distinction between process control events and product nonconformances).
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This gate determines the immediate containment action. A Product NCR focuses on quarantining parts. A Process NCR focuses on stabilizing the process and then determining the product impact.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: What is the disposition for the quarantined product?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Options: Rework (sand/buff, repaint), Strip & Recoat, Scrap, Use-As-Is (requires concession), or Request Customer Concession. Each path has its own procedure.
decision_gate_reference_table.who_decides: Quality Manager or a designated alternate. This authority cannot be delegated lower.
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: Formal NCMR data, cost-benefit analysis, availability of approved rework procedures, customer requirements for concessions.
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: Typically within 24 hours of NCR creation to avoid production delays.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: Strip & Recoat is a common but expensive option for high-value parts with e-coat or powder coat. Simple buffing/polishing is more common for minor surface defects in liquid clearcoat.
decision_gate_reference_table.standard_basis: IATF 16949 Clause 8.7.1.1 (Customer authorization for concession); AIAG CQI-12 Clause 1.10 (Rework) & 1.16 (Disposition Authority).
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This is a critical control point. The decision to 'Use-As-Is' or request a concession requires formal, documented customer approval *before* the product is shipped. Rework must follow a separate, documented process.

decision_gate_reference_table.gate_question: Is a systemic Corrective Action required to prevent recurrence?
decision_gate_reference_table.possible_answers_and_routing: Yes: Initiate a formal Corrective Action Report (CAR), often using an 8D or 5-Why methodology. No: Document the justification (e.g., isolated incident, human error with retraining completed) and close the NCR.
decision_gate_reference_table.information_required: NCR data, historical data on similar events, frequency of occurrence, risk analysis (e.g., FMEA RPN).
decision_gate_reference_table.timing: During the NCR review process, typically within a few days of the event.
decision_gate_reference_table.coating_specific_variations: Chronic low-level issues like minor orange peel (liquid spray) or fluctuating bath conductivity (e-coat) are prime candidates for a formal CAR to investigate systemic root causes.
decision_gate_reference_table.notes: This gate determines whether the event is treated as a one-off incident or a symptom of a larger problem. A common failure mode in quality systems is closing NCRs without adequate consideration for systemic corrective action, leading to recurring problems.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: ≥1 nonconformance on a customer-designated Safety or Critical Characteristic (e.g., Ford's inverted delta symbol ∇, GM's 'Delta C').
escalation_rule_reference_table.to_level: Critical NCR / Immediate Containment / Customer Notification
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Part/defect data linked to a master list of designated critical characteristics.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: Applies to all technologies. Critical characteristics in coating often relate to minimum film thickness for corrosion resistance, adhesion properties, or safety-related markings.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: ≥1 defect of any kind is found by the GP-12/EPC containment inspection station.
escalation_rule_reference_table.to_level: Formal Reaction Plan / GP-12 Clock Reset / Potential CS1 Escalation
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Documented inspection results from the dedicated GP-12/EPC station.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: A single defect, whether it's a dirt inclusion in liquid spray or a thin spot in e-coat, constitutes a failure of the standard production control plan.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: Systemic failure to resolve issues under GP-12 or CS1; a customer plant disruption caused by a defect; repeated nonconformances reaching the customer.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Supplier performance metrics, PRR history, severity of the new issue, GP-12/CS1 performance data.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This is a major escalation for a coating supplier, indicating their internal quality controls have fundamentally failed.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: A process parameter violates a standard SPC rule, such as: 1 point > 3 sigma from the mean; 2 of 3 consecutive points > 2 sigma; 4 of 5 consecutive points > 1 sigma; or 9 consecutive points on one side of the mean.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Real-time or periodic measurements of a key process parameter (e.g., oven temperature, bath pH, paint viscosity).
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: Critical for monitoring e-coat bath chemistry, pretreatment concentrations, and cure oven temperatures across all technologies.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: An EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) or CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) chart detects a small, sustained shift in the process mean (e.g., < 1.5 sigma) that would not trigger standard SPC rules.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Real-time or periodic measurements of a key process parameter.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: Essential for detecting the slow degradation of an e-coat bath, gradual solvent loss in liquid paint, or drift in powder reclaim ratios before they cause defects.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: The calculated process capability (Ppk) for a key characteristic is less than 1.33.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Sufficient measurement data from a key product characteristic (e.g., film thickness, gloss) to calculate a statistically valid Ppk.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This is a hard, numeric trigger that forces containment for processes that are not capable of consistently meeting requirements.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: The number of occurrences of the same defect type exceeds a predefined limit within a set timeframe or number of parts (e.g., '≥3 events of the same defect type in 5 production days').
escalation_rule_reference_table.auto_vs_manual: Auto (if system is configured) or Manual (via data review)
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: A timestamped event log with consistently categorized defect types.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: Often used to escalate chronic but individually minor cosmetic issues like dirt inclusions, minor orange peel, or small sags.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: Any formal complaint (e.g., PRR, SCAR, 8D request) is received from a customer.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Record of the formal communication from the customer.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This is a top-priority escalation that requires an immediate response, typically within 24 hours, including a containment plan.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: A required assessment fails to meet its specified tolerance. For example, a System Accuracy Test (SAT) on an oven shows a difference > ±5.0°C (±10.0°F), or a Temperature Uniformity Survey (TUS) falls outside limits.
escalation_rule_reference_table.source: AIAG CQI-12 (Special Process: Coating System Assessment)
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: The formal SAT or TUS report showing a pass/fail status.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This is a critical equipment-based trigger. A failed TUS renders an oven non-compliant and it cannot be used for production until corrected and resurveyed.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: While on a reduced monitoring schedule (justified by ≥30 consecutive in-control points), a single data point falls outside of the control limits.
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Process parameter measurements and the status of the current monitoring plan.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This rule ensures that any sign of instability immediately returns the process to a state of heightened scrutiny.

escalation_rule_reference_table.trigger_logic: A supplier's overall quality performance metric (e.g., GM's PRR rate, BMW's PPM rate) exceeds a contractually defined action limit over a rolling time window.
escalation_rule_reference_table.to_level: Formal Supplier Review / Increased Audit Frequency / SQIP
escalation_rule_reference_table.data_required: Aggregated data on total defects/complaints and total parts shipped over the defined period.
escalation_rule_reference_table.coating_specific_notes: This is a high-level business escalation that aggregates all types of coating defects into a single performance score, triggering management-level attention.

citations.title: International Automotive Task Force IATF 16949:2016
excerpts:
horization for**
**concession**
The organization shall obtain a customer concession or deviation permit prior to further
processing whenever the product or manufacturing process is different from that which is
currently approved.
The organization shall obtain customer authorization pri or to further processing for “use as is”
and **rework** **for** **repair (see 8.7.1.5)** **dispositions** of nonconforming product. If sub-
components are reused in the manufacturing process, that sub-component reuse shall be
clearly communicated to the customer in the concession or deviation perm

citations.title: GM 1927 28 Early Production Containment
excerpts: Early Production Containment (EPC) is to be used for all pre-production, production, service and accessory part requirements that;

Early Production Containment requires a Pre-Launch Control Plan as part of PPAP submission that is a significant enhancement to
the supplier's production control plan and raises the confidence level to ensure that all products shipped will meet GM’s
requirements.

citations.title: GP-12 Early Production Containment Guide | PDF
excerpts:
4.0 EXIT CRITERIA:
Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the
supplier is unable to meet the exit criteria or the supplier’s GP
-12 plan continues to identify non-conformances the supplier shall continue the necessary containment measures to insulate the customer until the quality concerns have been resolved to the satisfaction of both the
supplier and the customer and the supplier’s Production Control Plan is validated.
If a PRR is issued for a feature not covered on GP -12, then the feature must be added to GP-12.
If a PRR is issued for a feature covered on GP- 12, then the supplier shall be entered into formal Controlled Shipping 1 (CS1).

citations.title: IATF 16949 - Customer Specific Requirements
excerpts:
The organization’s documented problem-solving process shall include:
1. Tracking of issues through closure.
2. Daily review of issues by a multi-disciplined team including plant management.
3. Daily reviews are documented.
4. All levels of the organization are included in the problem-solving process.
5. Robust method to identify the verifiable root cause(s) of each issue.
6. Timely closure of corrective action(s) including exit criteria.
7. Initial containment is well documented using a containment worksheet or similar
The organization shall perform quality focused checks on each shift.

citations.title: IATF 16949 - Customer Specific Requirements
excerpts: The organization shall perform quality focused checks on each shift.

outputBasis.reasoning: The most directly relevant excerpts are those that articulate the formal problem-solving and nonconforming-material workflows used across automotive supply chains. Excerpts describing the problem-solving process in IATF 16949 (8.2/10.x sections and the sanctioned interpretations) provide the backbone for how events are classified, routed, and closed, including the requirement for containment and corrective action and the need to involve multiple levels of the organization. For example, material in excerpts detailing the GP-12 EPC/Exit criteria and containment procedure (GP-12 EPC) describes the escalation path from pre-launch containment to exit criteria and containment actions, which directly align with the field value’s notion of a staged containment and escalation workflow. Other excerpts discuss the GP-12 EPC itself, including the need for containment plans, exit criteria, and escalation when issues persist, which supports the “containment” and “escalation” parts of the field value’s event taxonomy. Additional excerpts discuss Ford CSR and GM/Supplier Quality-related requirements that specify customer notification, 8D/8D-like problem solving, and escalation timelines, which map to the field value’s “Customer-originated events” and “Disposition/Root-Cause/Concession” pathways. The CQI-12 Coating System Assessment excerpts provide context on process-control requirements, traceability, and the organization-wide responsibility for continual improvement, which underlie how events are classified, how process controls are monitored, and how information is cascaded through the organization to prevent recurrence. The combination of GP-12 EPC, IATF 16949 8.7 (nonconforming outputs), and CQI-12 guidance helps connect the field value’s event taxonomy with concrete classification tiers, discovery, disposition, and escalation logic, including who makes classifications and when, what information is needed, and how containment and corrective actions are documented and closed.

outputBasis.reasoning: The most relevant material comes from sources that explicitly describe the disposition workflow and the need for customer authorization in the context of nonconforming coating products. Statements stating that disposition decisions require customer authorization or concessions align with the user-valued field’s emphasis on mandatory approvals before further processing and the distinction between rework (to original specs) and repair (to an acceptable state that may not meet origin specs). References to GP-12/EPC and WERS illustrate concrete OEM containment and disposition pathways used during launches and in supplier processes, which reinforces the governance layer that surrounds disposition decisions. The CQI-12 references emphasize traceability and the use of separate rework controls or forms, which supports the idea that disposition activities are tightly controlled and traceable. Additional items discuss the broader problem-solving framework (root-cause, closure, containment) that underpins the disposition decision, ensuring any corrective actions are properly tracked and closed. Finally, OEM-specific requirements (GM AAR, Ford CSR) provide concrete manifestations of customer approval and escalation paths that constrain disposition decisions in practice, matching the user’s scenario of sequential, policy-driven disposition tied to initial classification.

citations.title: Nonconformance: Definition, Types, Causes, and Process
excerpts: Nonconformances are typically categorized as critical, major, or minor, based on risk to product quality, patient/end user safety, and compliance.
The purpose of classification is to ensure consistent risk-based prioritization and regulatory alignment.
The assigned classification determines incident reporting obligations, resource allocation, and the scope of CAPA activities.
QA generally leads nonconformance classification with cross-functional input as needed.
classification step formally determines the risk level of the nonconformance.

citations.title: International Automotive Task Force IATF 16949:2016
excerpts:

citations.title: GM 1927 28 Early Production Containment
excerpts: Early Production Containment (EPC) is to be used for all pre-production, production, service and accessory part requirements that;

Early Production Containment requires a Pre-Launch Control Plan as part of PPAP submission that is a significant enhancement to
the supplier's production control plan and raises the confidence level to ensure that all products shipped will meet GM’s
requirements.
The purpose of EPC is to:
Validate the supplier’s production control plan

Protect GM assembly and manufacturing centers and service part warehouses from quality non-conformances during
critical periods including Launch.

Document the supplier's efforts to verify control of its processes during start-up, acceleration, after revisions to the
manufacturing process, or when manufacturing runs are shut down for extended period of downtime, critical engineering
change or if supplier is providing new product during Launch.

citations.title: IATF 16949:2016 Clause 9.1.2.1 Customer satisfaction - PRETESH BISWAS
excerpts:
Section Title: IATF 16949:2016 Clause 9.1.2.1 Customer satisfaction
Content:
You must continually gather information (about these requirements), capable of being analyzed and evaluated to determine how well you performed them. There are all kinds of performance indicators for design, manufacture, delivery, etc. Gather information on these indicators from both the customer as well from internal processes. You are expected to have a process that defines your customer satisfaction indicators; frequency and method of data collection; summarization, review and evaluation of data; actions to improve, timeline, responsibility and follow-up You must continuously review this customer feedback to ensure you maintain and improve your customer satisfaction rating. ‘Delivered part delivery performance’ relates to reduction in PPM defect rates. ‘Customer disruptions’ relate to temporary interruption or shutdown of your customers production or service operations that was caused by product quality or delivery problems from your organization. ‘Delivery schedule performance’ relates to product or service delivery that is not early or late, but just in time based on customer scheduling requirements. Premium freight whether paid by you or the customer to expedite on-time delivery is a non-value added cost that must be prevented. You are required to track premium freight costs and take corrective action if it is a significant cost or occurs frequently.

While BMW initially used spray guns to apply the powder clear coat, these were
replaced in 2002 by powder bells, which allowed higher flow rates (less applicators)
and led to a lower defect rate. Also in 2002, BMW introduced DDF (Digitale
Dichtstrom F¨orderung, Ramseier Technologies AG) pumps for powder dosing
and supply, which significantly reduced the consumption of air in the fluidization
system improving the dosing accuracy and transfer efficiency

The standard method of applying powder
coatings requires fluidization of the powder to make it pneumatically transportable.
The graph clearly shows that finer powders are also possible with higher densities.
The higher the average particle size, the lower the tendency to agglomerate.

Flow and TE have the opposite demands on powder size. In other words, the
finer the particles, the better are the flow properties obtained.

The CQI-12 excerpts further distinguish process-control events from product nonconformances, which is central to the field value’s aim of mapping event types, routing logic, and disposition governance. Taken together, the strongest support comes from explicit clause references and process descriptions (IATF 16949 stipulations), followed by CQI-12 process versus product event delineations, and then OEM-specific implementations that illustrate concrete escalation and containment practices. outputBasis.reasoning:
The field value posits a pragmatic two-level quality event classification (informal 'Quality Alerts' vs formal 'Non-Conformance Reports' NCRs) as common in automotive coating operations, anchored by industry standards and OEM-specific requirements. It also ties classification to containment/disposition workflows and escalation logic, informed by IATF 16949, CQI-12, and CSRs from OEMs like GM, Ford, Toyota, and BMW. The following elements from the excerpts support these assertions:
- The IATF 16949 oriented Clauses emphasize a documented problem-solving process and control of nonconforming outputs, which underpins structured classifications and disposition workflows. This aligns with a two-tier approach where initial detection may be informal (alerts) and escalation to formal NCRs triggers containment and corrective actions. The presence of a formal problem-solving framework (tracked issues through closure, daily cross-functional reviews, and robust root-cause methods) substantiates the need for formalized classification and escalation channels. In addition, sanctioned interpretations reinforce the expectation of a structured approach to containment, root-cause, and corrective actions. This supports the claim that industry practice embeds a tiered workflow anchored by standards, while allowing supplier discretion in model design.
- The CQI-12 material explicitly describes a coating-system assessment requirement and mentions process controls, documented rework procedures, and reaction plans. This corroborates the notion that OEM-aligned (and multi-process) assessments pressure suppliers to implement formal processes around event classification, containment, and disposition pathways. The material also emphasizes the need for assessment forms, process control, and evidence, which reinforces the idea that classification and disposition are codified components of a broader coating-management system rather than ad hoc practices.
- OEM customer requirements (GM, Ford, Toyota, BMW) consistently refer to specific containment and escalation constructs (GP-12 Early Production Containment, EPC; CS II/CS I for containment; Ford Q1 and testing regimes; GM’s GP-12 lineage and Ppk-based thresholds). These sources illustrate concrete, industry-standard containment paths and escalation gates that sit atop classification logic. They demonstrate that while there is room for supplier-defined models, industry practice leans on well-defined escalation ladders and containment steps that are triggered by observed deviations or performance concerns. This directly supports the field’s emphasis on a two-tier-ish model: an initial informal alert, followed by a formal, OEM-backed containment/disposition regime.
- The Ford CSR material discusses the necessity of PPAP-related controls, appearance-related requirements, and explicit escalation and notification expectations (e.g., 24h customer notification, 15-day corrective actions), which illustrate how OEMs enforce rapid escalation and formal disposition steps after initial observations. This aligns with the two-tier framing where informal observations escalate to formal processes under OEM guidance.
- GM GP-12 EPC/CS constructs and the GM 8D-style problem-solving emphasis show a staged containment and escalation pathway, including early containment and subsequent actions, which map cleanly onto a two-layer model (informal alerting plus formal NCR-like containment). The references to containment, exit criteria, and escalation levels provide concrete mechanics for how events move from initial detection to formal disposition, aligning with the discussed two-level approach.
- The excerpts also touch on calibration, measurement-system concerns, and process-control-appearance aspects (e.g., 8D, containment, traceability), which are consistent with a holistic system where event classification is inseparable from disposition and corrective action planning, especially in multi-technology coating environments.
- In sum, the excerpts collectively support the here-described landscape: an industry that relies on a structured standard-driven framework (IATF 16949, CQI-12, CSRs), a tiered classification ecosystem that can be simplified into a two-level practical model on the shop floor, explicit containment escalations (GP-12/EPC, CS levels, QPR/SCMS), and OEM-imposed disposition and documentation requirements. They also confirm that disposition decisions (rework, scrap, etc.) are bounded by customer approvals and by formal processes when conformance is questioned, which matches the field’s emphasis on customer-concession and formal corrective-action loops.

Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the

excerpts: Section Title: Level 1 and Level 2 Controlled Shipping > Controlled Shipping – Level 1
There are two main levels of controlled shipping and some customers also require a third level.
Level 1 requires the supplier to contain the suspect product and perform a redundant 100% inspection process.
This process may be done by the supplier's employees, though some suppliers routinely employ an outside third-party inspection company to perform this service.
QLS has trained associates with experience in conducting controlled shipping projects.
The second step of Level 1 is to identify the root cause of the defect and incorporate irreversible corrective actions.

Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the

the supplier's production control plan and raises the confidence level to ensure that all products shipped will meet GM’s
requirements.
The purpose of EPC is to:
Validate the supplier’s production control plan

Protect GM assembly and manufacturing centers and service part warehouses from quality non-conformances during
critical periods including Launch.

Document the supplier's efforts to verify control of its processes during start-up, acceleration, after revisions to the
manufacturing process, or when manufacturing runs are shut down for extended period of downtime, critical engineering
change or if supplier is providing new product during Launch.

During Launch Phase, EPC shall be implemented at start of first GM plant assembly builds that requires PPAP status. Normally, 1 st
builds will be APPV builds that requires NS PPAP. EPC shall remain in place until 90 calendar days after SORP to confirm
production control plan has been validated.

To indicate compliance with the EPC requirements, attach to each shipping label a green circular, sticker,
approximately 25mm in diameter, signed by the staff person accountable to insure proper implementation of.

This aligns directly with the containment-first philosophy implied by the field value, where a single defect at the containment station constitutes a failure of the standard production control plan and initiates a defined escalation path. Additional excerpts explain that EPC requires a Pre-Launch Control Plan and is applied during first builds or NS PPAP, reinforcing the containment-led governance during critical phases. The presence of green/containment labeling and the focus on error containment during launch further corroborate the intended control mindset that a single incident prompts formal action and potential escalation. Taken together, these excerpts collectively support the existence of an auto-triggered containment mechanism, the use of documented inspection results as the data input, and the escalation toward a formal reaction plan or clock reset, which matches the described finegrained field value.

citations.title: GP-12 Early Production Containment Guide | PDF
excerpts:
4.0 EXIT CRITERIA:
Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the
supplier is unable to meet the exit criteria or the supplier’s GP
-12 plan continues to identify non-conformances the supplier shall continue the necessary containment measures to insulate the customer until the quality concerns have been resolved to the satisfaction of both the
supplier and the customer and the supplier’s Production Control Plan is validated.
If a PRR is issued for a feature not covered on GP -12, then the feature must be added to GP-12.
If a PRR is issued for a feature covered on GP- 12, then the supplier shall be entered into formal Controlled Shipping 1 (CS1).

citations.title: GP-12 Early Production Containment Guide | PDF
excerpts:
4.0 EXIT CRITERIA:
Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the
supplier is unable to meet the exit criteria or the supplier’s GP
-12 plan continues to identify non-conformances the supplier shall continue the necessary containment measures to insulate the customer until the quality concerns have been resolved to the satisfaction of both the
supplier and the customer and the supplier’s Production Control Plan is validated.
If a PRR is issued for a feature not covered on GP -12, then the feature must be added to GP-12.
If a PRR is issued for a feature covered on GP- 12, then the supplier shall be entered into formal Controlled Shipping 1 (CS1).

citations.title: GP-12 Early Production Containment Guide | PDF
excerpts:
4.0 EXIT CRITERIA:
Supplier will be eligible to exit GP-12 after validating the effectiveness of Process Control Plan and meeting the criteria listed below. If the
supplier is unable to meet the exit criteria or the supplier’s GP
-12 plan continues to identify non-conformances the supplier shall continue the necessary containment measures to insulate the customer until the quality concerns have been resolved to the satisfaction of both the
supplier and the customer and the supplier’s Production Control Plan is validated.
If a PRR is issued for a feature not covered on GP -12, then the feature must be added to GP-12.
If a PRR is issued for a feature covered on GP- 12, then the supplier shall be entered into formal Controlled Shipping 1 (CS1).

requirements.

citations.title: GM 1927 28 Early Production Containment
excerpts:
During Launch Phase, EPC shall be implemented at start of first GM plant assembly builds that requires PPAP status. Normally, 1 st
builds will be APPV builds that requires NS PPAP. EPC shall remain in place until 90 calendar days after SORP to confirm
production control plan has been validated.

Document the supplier's efforts to verify control of its processes during start-up, acceleration, after revisions to the
manufacturing process, or when manufacturing runs are shut down for extended period of downtime, critical engineering
change or if supplier is providing new product during Launch.

Early Production Containment requires a Pre-Launch Control Plan as part of PPAP submission that is a significant enhancement to
the supplier's production control plan and raises the confidence level to ensure that all products shipped will meet GM’s
requirements.
Early Production Containment (EPC) is to be used for all pre-production, production, service and accessory part requirements that;

Protect GM assembly and manufacturing centers and service part warehouses from quality non-conformances during
critical periods including Launch.
The purpose of EPC is to:
Validate the supplier’s production control plan

To indicate compliance with the EPC requirements, attach to each shipping label a green circular, sticker,
approximately 25mm in diameter, signed by the staff person accountable to insure proper implementation of.

citations.title: GM 1927 28 Early Production Containment
excerpts:
Protect GM assembly and manufacturing centers and service part warehouses from quality non-conformances during
critical periods including Launch.
Early Production Containment (EPC) is to be used for all pre-production, production, service and accessory part requirements that;

Early Production Containment requires a Pre-Launch Control Plan as part of PPAP submission that is a significant enhancement to
the supplier's production control plan and raises the confidence level to ensure that all products shipped will meet GM’s
requirements.
Validate the supplier’s production control plan

During Launch Phase, EPC shall be implemented at start of first GM plant assembly builds that requires PPAP status. Normally, 1 st
builds will be APPV builds that requires NS PPAP. EPC shall remain in place until 90 calendar days after SORP to confirm
production control plan has been validated.

To indicate compliance with the EPC requirements, attach to each shipping label a green circular, sticker,
approximately 25mm in diameter, signed by the staff person accountable to insure proper implementation of.

Taken together, these excerpts support a structured, multi-level escalation model in coating/automotive quality events, including the existence of a third level in practice, and the kinds of decisions and approvals that escalate events upward.

A second supporting element is the provision that after a customer complaint, the standard requires analysis of interactions within the system, which implies formal investigation and escalation beyond routine checks. The combination of stop-shipment mandates, post-complaint analysis, and the requirement for customer authorization on certain dispositions provides concrete mechanisms by which a major escalation would be enacted in coating operations, supporting the field value’s claim of a fundamental quality-control failure driving escalation.

The GM Early Production Containment (EPC) documents outline GM’s approach to controlling quality during pre-launch and early production, including the need for containment, a pre-launch control plan as part of PPAP, and enhancements to ensure products meet GM requirements. These excerpts collectively illustrate GM’s emphasis on proactive containment, validation of supplier production control plans, and clear communication of any deviations or approvals required before continuing production. This aligns with the concept of OEM customer-specific requirements by defining what GM expects suppliers to implement to prevent nonconformances from affecting GM manufacturing and to secure customer authorization when deviations or use-as-is/repairs may be involved. The cited GM EPC material explicitly states the purpose of EPC, its application across pre-production and production phases, and the requirement to attach containment and control plan assurances to shipments, which are core elements of GM’s customer-specific quality governance. The accompanying GM EPC excerpts discuss the need to validate a supplier’s production control plan and to apply containment during high-risk periods (launch, acceleration, post-revisions, downtime), which exemplifies how GM prescribes escalation and containment workflows that suppliers must follow to align with GM’s quality expectations. In addition, IATF 16949-related excerpts about concessions and rework for nonconforming products provide context on how OEMs require customer concessions or deviation permits prior to reprocessing, which is relevant to GM’s broader framework for handling deviations and ensuring customer-approved paths when necessary. Taken together, these GM-focused containment and escalation references strongly support the notion of “GM Customer Specific Requirements” as an OEM-driven governance layer for quality event handling in coating and manufacturing supply chains, illustrating the mechanisms by which GM requires control, escalation, and customer approval pathways. The non-GM OEM and standard references provide supportive contrast and help situate GM requirements within the broader standards landscape, but the GM EPC content is the primary, most direct evidence of GM-specific expectations.

executive_summary.description: A high-level summary of the entire research report, covering the key models, standards, and practices for quality event classification in automotive coating operations.

quality_event_classification_models_surv.description: Part 1, Section 1: A comprehensive survey of recognized models for classifying quality events, including tiered systems (minor/major/critical), event-type routing, source-based classification, and risk-based models. Analysis includes their logic, use cases, strengths, and weaknesses.

iatf_16949_analysis.description: Analysis of IATF 16949 requirements for quality event classification, highlighting mandatory rules and supplier discretion.

cqi_12_analysis.description: Analysis of how CQI-12 addresses process control events versus product nonconformances and its mandated escalation mechanisms.

oem_supplier_manuals_analysis.description: Analysis of specific requirements from GM, Ford, Toyota, and BMW supplier quality manuals regarding event handling and reporting.

iso_9001_analysis.description: Analysis of baseline ISO 9001:2015 clauses on nonconforming outputs and corrective action.

full_taxonomy_of_quality_events_analysis.description: Part 1, Section 3: A mapping of the complete landscape of quality event types in coating operations, including product defects, process deviations, equipment issues, material excursions, customer/supplier events, containment states, and more.

powder_coating_analysis.description: Analysis of unique event types, behaviors, and classification considerations for powder coating operations.

e_coat_analysis.description: Analysis of unique event types, behaviors, and classification considerations for e-coat (electrodeposition) operations.

liquid_spray_paint_analysis.description: Analysis of unique event types, behaviors, and classification considerations for liquid spray paint operations.

disposition_in_coating_operations_analys.description: Part 1, Section 5: An explanation of how disposition decisions (use-as-is, rework, scrap, etc.) interact with event classification, including when customer approval is mandatory and the risks of rework loops.

escalation_and_accumulation_logic_analys.description: Part 1, Section 6: A research summary of how quality systems handle the transition from routine observations to formal investigations, covering frequency, trend, severity, and cost-based triggers, and how to handle chronic low-level problems.

decision_gate_design_analysis.description: Part 1, Section 7: An analysis of the decision logic at the point of event creation, including the sequence of questions asked, responsible roles, and information required to classify an event.

implementation_evidence_analysis.description: Part 1, Section 8: A summary of documented case studies, common failure modes (too simple, too complex), operator adoption patterns, and data quality issues when implementing quality event classification systems.

comparative_analysis_of_classification_m.description: Part 1 Conclusion: A comparative analysis of which classification models are best suited for multi-technology coating operations at various scales, with a clear assessment of strengths, weaknesses, and fit criteria for each model.

event_category.description: Top-level grouping for the event (e.g., Product, Process, Equipment, Customer).

event_type.description: The specific name of the quality event type.

definition.description: A concise, one-sentence definition of the event.

typical_trigger.description: The common cause or action that leads to the identification of this event.

typical_discoverer.description: The role that usually finds this type of event (e.g., operator, inspector, lab tech, customer).

typical_severity.description: The general default severity level (e.g., Low, Medium, High, Critical).

typical_disposition_path.description: The most common disposition action taken for this event type in coating operations.

escalation_triggers.description: Conditions or events that would cause this event to escalate to a higher-level investigation.

coating_technology.description: The coating technologies to which this event applies (e.g., Powder, E-Coat, Liquid, All).

technology_specific_notes.description: Notes on how this event behaves differently across various coating types.

relevant_standard_clause.description: The specific clause from IATF 16949, CQI-12, or OEM requirements that applies to this event.

classification_model_mapping.description: How this event type maps into the major classification models (e.g., Minor/Major/Critical).

quality_event_type_master_table.description: Part 2, Table A: A structured reference table of all identified quality event types. Each entry will include: Event Category, Event Type, Definition, Typical Trigger, Typical Discoverer, Typical Severity, Typical Disposition Path, Escalation Triggers, Coating Technology applicability, Technology-Specific Notes, Relevant Standard Clause, and Classification Model Mapping.

gate_number.description: The sequential number of the decision gate.

gate_question.description: The yes/no or multiple-choice question asked at this decision point.

possible_answers_and_routing.description: The available answer options and where each one routes to (e.g., next gate or final classification).

who_decides.description: The role that typically makes the decision at this gate (e.g., operator, supervisor, quality engineer).

information_required.description: The data or evidence needed to answer the gate question.

timing.description: When this decision is typically made (e.g., at discovery, within X hours, at shift end).

coating_specific_variations.description: Notes on where the decision logic or answer differs by coating technology.

standard_basis.description: The standard or industry practice from which this gate's logic is derived.

notes.description: Practical considerations, common mistakes, or details on handling edge cases.

decision_gate_reference_table.description: Part 2, Table B: A sequential reference table outlining the classification decision logic. Each entry will represent a gate and include: Gate #, Gate Question, Possible Answers and routing, Who Decides, Information Required, Timing, Coating-Specific Variations, Standard Basis, and Notes on edge cases or common mistakes.

rule_number.description: A sequential number for the escalation rule.

rule_name.description: A descriptive name for the rule.

trigger_type.description: The type of trigger (e.g., Frequency, Trend, Severity, Cost, Customer, Pattern).

trigger_logic.description: The specific logic of the rule (e.g., '>=3 events of same defect type in 5 production days').

source.description: The origin of the rule (e.g., standard, industry practice, case study).

from_level.description: The initial level or tier of event that this rule applies to.

to_level.description: The level or tier that the event escalates to when the rule is triggered.

auto_vs_manual.description: Indicates whether the trigger is automatic or flags for a human decision.

data_required.description: The data the system needs to evaluate this rule.

coating_specific_notes.description: Any considerations specific to different coating technologies.

escalation_rule_reference_table.description: Part 2, Table C: A structured reference table of all escalation and accumulation triggers found in the research. Each entry will include: Rule #, Rule Name, Trigger Type (Frequency, Trend, Severity, etc.), Trigger Logic, Source, From Level, To Level, Auto vs. Manual trigger, Data Required, and Coating-Specific Notes.
